claimed_contribution: The paper introduces a multiple-goal-based choice model (MGBCM) that explicitly embeds simultaneous multiple-goal pursuit and context-driven goal-weight adaptation in a random-choice framework.
It provides empirical evidence of within-choice goal-weight adjustment based on goal attainability of the presented assortment, complementing prior work on across-occasion goal revision.
The model is designed to recover latent goals from choice data without requiring a priori specification of the number or nature of goals.
context: The empirical setting is consumer choice of point-and-shoot digital cameras. Data come from Canadian online-panel respondents making repeated discrete choices over camera assortments with varying attribute profiles and choice-set structures.
design_and_data: The authors develop a multiobjective optimization choice model in which each latent goal has its own attainment function, prior goal weights depend on individual characteristics, and adapted goal weights depend on context-specific expected maximum goal attainability. They estimate the model by maximum likelihood on a main survey of 1,890 Canadian adults who owned a point-and-shoot camera; each respondent completed 8 discrete-choice tasks (choice sets of size 2, 4, or 8) using full product profiles. They compare MGBCM fit against latent class and hidden Markov alternatives using BIC, and use self-reported goals only for validation of latent-goal interpretation. For out-of-sample validation, they use a separate sample of 1,002 respondents facing 16 tasks built from 10 real market cameras with varied availability and a wider price range.
main_findings: In-sample, the optimal MGBCM (five latent goals) outperforms the optimal latent class model on BIC by 165 points, and removing the adaptive archetype worsens fit.
Out-of-sample on a different design and sample, MGBCM substantially outperforms the latent class model (BIC 41,373 vs. 43,613), consistent with the predictive value of modeling adaptation.

Estimated adaptation patterns are behaviorally plausible: consumers with more objective camera knowledge show greater adaptation tendency, and only some latent goals display strong across-context weight variation.
one_line_summary: The study develops and tests a multiple-goal-based discrete-choice model that infers latent goals and within-choice context-driven goal-weight adaptation, and shows superior fit and out-of-sample prediction versus utility-based latent class alternatives in digital camera choice.
relation_to_prior_work: extends utility-based random-choice and latent-class approaches by modeling choice as multiobjective optimization over latent goals with explicit context-dependent goal weighting. It also extends goal-based consumer research focused on across-occasion goal revision by showing within-choice adaptation tied to assortment-specific attainability.
research_gap: Prior choice models in the random utility tradition do not explicitly model consumers pursuing multiple goals and adapting goal weights within a single choice context. Existing goal-based consumer work largely examined goal-weight changes across occasions (e.g., progress or satiation) rather than within-choice adaptation to the presented assortment.
research_question: Can consumer choice be better modeled by representing it as simultaneous pursuit of multiple latent goals with context-driven adaptation of goal weights within a choice occasion? Does such a model improve empirical fit, prediction, and managerial inference relative to standard utility-based latent class formulations?
